title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their learning experiences. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Never Stop Learning: Four People on Education

texts.D:
### Sanjay, 22, Engineering Student
I'm in the final year of my engineering degree and I have a complicated relationship with formal education. [44]I love the theory and the problem-solving aspects of my course, but I've found the emphasis on exams deeply frustrating.[/44] You can memorise the right answers and pass every test without really understanding anything. [48]The learning I value most has come from a summer internship where I worked on real projects with real consequences.[/48] That experience taught me more in ten weeks than a year of lectures. I think universities need to integrate practical experience much more systematically into their programmes.
texts.C:
### Mei, 58, Retired Teacher
[45]I taught secondary school English for thirty years and I remain completely convinced that a great teacher can change a child's life.[/45] I've had former students contact me decades later to say that something I said in class stayed with them. That never stops being extraordinary. [49]The most important thing I tried to give my students was a love of learning for its own sake, not just for the sake of passing exams.[/49] [52]The hardest part of teaching was watching children who had the potential to thrive fail because of problems at home that the school system had no power to fix.[/52]
texts.A:
### Alex, 30, App Developer
[46]I am entirely self-taught – I never studied computer science formally but taught myself to code using online resources, tutorials, and communities.[/46] Everything I know about programming I learned by doing, often by making mistakes. [50]The internet has made it possible for anyone with determination to learn almost any skill for free, which I think is one of the most important developments of our time.[/50] [53]The only downside of self-directed learning is the lack of structure – it can be hard to know what to learn next or to recognise your own blind spots.[/53] I sometimes envy people who had a teacher to guide them through the fundamentals.
texts.B:
### Harriet, 44, Human Resources Manager
[43]I left school at sixteen with very few qualifications because I found the traditional classroom environment stifling.[/43] I learned far more through working than I ever did sitting at a desk. [47]In my late thirties, I decided to do a part-time degree while working full-time, which was one of the hardest things I've ever done but also the most rewarding.[/47] It completely changed how my colleagues saw me – and more importantly, how I saw myself. [51]I think the education system fails people who don't learn well in formal settings, and that's a problem society needs to address urgently.[/51]

questions.question: struggled in conventional education and left school without many qualifications?
questions.explanation: Harriet left school at sixteen with few qualifications because she found the classroom environment stifling.

questions.question: is critical of the way their current education measures knowledge?
questions.explanation: Sanjay finds the emphasis on exams frustrating because you can pass without truly understanding the material.

questions.question: expresses a strong belief in the power of an individual educator to make a lasting difference?
questions.explanation: Mei is completely convinced that a great teacher can change a child's life and has received proof from former students.

questions.question: acquired all of their professional knowledge without attending formal classes?
questions.explanation: Alex is entirely self-taught and never studied computer science formally, using online resources instead.

questions.question: returned to formal education as an adult while continuing to work?
questions.explanation: Harriet did a part-time degree in her late thirties while working full-time.

questions.question: found that practical work experience was more valuable than their academic study?
questions.explanation: Sanjay says his summer internship on real projects taught him more in ten weeks than a year of lectures.

questions.question: describes trying to inspire a genuine enthusiasm for knowledge beyond exam performance?
questions.explanation: Mei says the most important thing she tried to give students was a love of learning for its own sake.

questions.question: highlights technology as having made learning more accessible and democratic?
questions.explanation: Alex says the internet has made it possible for anyone with determination to learn almost any skill for free.

questions.question: argues that the education system lets down people who have particular learning styles?
questions.explanation: Harriet says the education system fails people who don't learn well in formal settings.

questions.question: mentions a source of frustration in their career that was beyond their control?
questions.explanation: Mei found it hardest to watch children fail due to problems at home that the school system couldn't fix.
